coredata.dc:title: Dual-emissive NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposites for visual and quantitative detection of Hg2+ and anti-counterfeiting application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Xiaogao
Xu, Xianzhu
Zhang, Qiyue
Wu, Yanli
Wang, Xingcong
Chen, Xi
Xiao, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a dual-emissive NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite was successfully fabricated by incorporating blue emissive Carbon dots into mesoporous popcorn shape NaGdF4:Eu nanoparticles with red emission by one step hydrothermal process. The ratio fluorescence signal (F470nm:F621nm) of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs solution exhibited a linear relation with Hg2+ concentration. A ratiometric fluoresent method for Hg2+ detection was established with a broad linear range from 0 to 300 µM, and LOD of 20.8 nM. Meanwhile, a smartphone assisted paper-based nanosensor for Hg2+ visual detection based on CDs and NaGdF4:Eu was constructed successfully. The linear range was between 0 to 300 µM, and the LOD was calculated to be 24.75 nM. Furthermore, the obtained ratio fluoresent probe and smartphone-assisted visual inspection platform were both applied for determination of Hg2+ from actual water samples. The recoveries in the actual water samples detection revealed excellent stability of the fluorescence probe and the smartphone-assisted detection platform based on the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite. Furthermore, the developed NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite can serve as fluorescent ink, exhibit unique properties for anti-counterfeiting. The developed nanocomposite have great potential in optical sensor and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Hg2+ detection
Smartphone-assisted platform
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The experiment for detection of Hg2+
Phase structure, morphology, and formation process
Hg2+ detection by NaGdF4:Eu/CDs ratiometric fluorescence probe
Hg2+ detection in water samples by fluorescence spectrophotometer and smartphone

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) X-ray diffraction analysis of the NaGdF4:Eu /CDs nanocomposite. (B) UV/VIS absorption spectroscopy of the samples. (C) XPS spectra of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs and CDs; (D, E) SEM images of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite; The corresponding elemental mapping of NaGdF4:Eu/ CDs nanocomposite: Gd (F), F (G), Na (H) , C (I), O (J), N (K), Eu (L). TEM images of CDs (M) and NaGdF4:Eu (N) and NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite (O).

(A)Excitation and emission spectra of the CDs aqueous solution; (B)Excitation and emission spectra of NaGdF4:Eu; (C) Emission spectrum of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs aqueous solution under 274 nm excitation (inset photographs show corresponding solutions under a 254 nm UV lamp); (D)Fluorescence response of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs (10 mg/mL, pH 7.0, reactime 5 min) to various concentrations of Hg2+;(E) Linear relation between Hg2+ concentrations (0 to 300µM) and emission intensity ratio (inset: photographs of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs solutions with various concentrations of Hg2+ under a 254 nm UV lamp.).

(A)The fluoresence photoes of NaGdF4:Eu/CD test paper after addition of different ions (300 µM); (B)fluorescence photoes of NaGdF4:Eu/CD test paper against Hg2+ (100 µM) and other ions (500 µM);(C) Selectively (blue bar) and anti-interference (purple bar) of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs test paper;(D) fitted curve of color ratio value (R/B) analyzed by smartphone Color Grab APP. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) the absorption peak of the UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples; (B) The fluorescence decay curve of the solution; Fluorescence photoes of the letters and butterfly writed with anti-counterfeit inks under daylight (1, 5); under 365 nm lamp (2,6); under 254 nm lamp (3,7), and under 254 nm with Hg2+ added(4,8), respectively.
Entire synthesis process of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite and its application for quantitative and visual detection of Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Dual-emissive NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposites for visual and quantitative detection of Hg2+ and anti-counterfeiting application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, Institute of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013 China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules
Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, Institute of Organic Chemistry, Jiangxi Science and Technology Normal University, Nanchang 330013, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330031 China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Jiangxi Normal University, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this manuscript, a dual-emissive NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite was successfully fabricated by embedding blue emissive Carbon dots into mesoporous popcorn shape NaGdF4: Eu nanoparticles with red emission by one step in-situ hydrothermal process. And a smartphone-assisted platform for visual and quantitative detection of Hg2+ was constructed by using the obtained nanocomposite, test paper and smartphone. Furthermore, the developed NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite can serve as fluorescent ink, exhibit unique properties for anti-counterfeiting.

Herein, a dual-emissive NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite was successfully fabricated by incorporating blue emissive Carbon dots into mesoporous popcorn shape NaGdF4:Eu nanoparticles with red emission by one step hydrothermal process. The ratio fluorescence signal (F470nm:F621nm) of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs solution exhibited a linear relation with Hg2+ concentration. A ratiometric fluoresent method for Hg2+ detection was established with a broad linear range from 0 to 300 µM, and LOD of 20.8 nM. Meanwhile, a smartphone assisted paper-based nanosensor for Hg2+ visual detection based on CDs and NaGdF4:Eu was constructed successfully. The linear range was between 0 to 300 µM, and the LOD was calculated to be 24.75 nM. Furthermore, the obtained ratio fluoresent probe and smartphone-assisted visual inspection platform were both applied for determination of Hg2+ from actual water samples. The recoveries in the actual water samples detection revealed excellent stability of the fluorescence probe and the smartphone-assisted detection platform based on the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite. Furthermore, the developed NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite can serve as fluorescent ink, exhibit unique properties for anti-counterfeiting. The developed nanocomposite have great potential in optical sensor and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury ions (Hg2+), one of the most toxic heavy metal ions and a widely distributed pollutant, have seriously affected the environment and human health [1,2]. Therefore, detecting Hg2+ in the environment and food is vital. Traditional detection methods such as atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [3,4], mass spectrometry [5,6], and colorimetry [7,8],have high accuracy and reliability, but their shortcomings such as complex operation, expensive equipment and long analysis time limit the application of these methods in field or remote detection.Thus, molecular imprinting technology [9–11], electrochemical analysis technology [12,13], surface enhanced Raman scattering [14], fluorescence analysis technology [15–19] are attracting more and more attention from researchers recently. Molecular imprinting technology has received widespread attention for its efficient and accurate identification of specific selected molecules, but its main drawback is the relatively complex preparation process and high cost [9,10]. Surface enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) has high selectivity and sensitivity, but poor substrate uniformity limits its practical applications [14]. In recent years, fluorescence detection has shown good performance in selectivity, sensitivity, and response speed, but further improvement is still needed in terms of stability and practical applications. To establish a stable, more convenient, and economical method for detecting Hg2+, carbon dots is a favorable choice.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel fluorescent material, have attracted increasing interest due to their unique optical properties, low cost and toxicity, chemical stability, and ease of functionalization [20–22]. Recently, many efforts have been made to develop potential applications of CDs, including in fields such as biological imaging [23], chemical sensing [24], photocatalysis [25], light-emitting diodes [26], and Anti −counterfeiting [27]. As a transition metal, Hg2+ is electron-deficient and can be specifically captured by electron-rich groups (such as –COOH and –OH) on the surface of CDs, causing changes in their surface state and quenching their fluorescence [28]. On this basis, a convenient and economical Hg2+ detection platform can be constructed by detecting the changes of the CDs fluorescence intensity caused by Hg2+ [28–31]. However, a single-emission probe based on CDs used for qualitative or quantitative detection of targets is usually interfered with the substrate and the microenvironment of the probe, which substantially reduces its detection ability [32,33]. Constructing a ratiometric fluorescence probe is an ideal way to compensate for the inherent defects of a single signal sensor. The effects of environmental interference can be neutralized by measuring the ratio of the double-emission fluorescence intensity of different wavelengths in a single excitation. Compared with traditional fluorescence analysis methods, ratiometric analysis self-calibrates for signals by simultaneously recording changes in fluorescence signals of 2 or more different wavelengths. Thereby, more sensitive and reliable detection of target analytes can be achieved. Recently, researchers have reported some interesting Carbon Dot-Based Composites ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting Hg2+ [33–35] .

As an important rare earth element, Eu3+ has a narrow emission spectrum, high color purity and high conversion efficiency [36–38]. Many studies have been focused on fabricating composites composed of CDs and Eu3+, and expending the application in various fields [38–46]. These composites were reported to have emission peaks of both CDs and Eu3+ without interfering with each other, making them potentially ideal for ratiometric probe. Usually, CD-Eu composites can be obtained by modifying Eu complex with functionalized CDs [36] or embedding CDs into Eu3+ doped matrix (such as Eu3+ metal–organic frameworks [38–43], Eu3+ doped polymer [44,45], Eu3+ doped inorganic material [46–48], etc). For example, Gan et al. have prepared a dual-emission fluorescence sensor for ultrasensitive sensing Hg2+ by combining blue emissive carbon dots and red emissive europium complexes (Ad-Eu-DPA) [36]. Zhang etal. [41] have prepared a fluorescence probe for visual quantitative detection of Ag+ by embedding CDs into Eu3+ metal–organic frameworks. However, there are very limited reports concerning of the nanosensors based on CDs and Eu3+ doped inorganic materials [46–48]. The Eu3+ doped inorganic materials are relatively stable and are not easy to react chemically with the external environment. In contrast, organic complexes and MOF materials may be more susceptible to environmental factors, degradation or deterioration. ‌ It can be reasonably inferred that incorporating CDs into inorganic materials can improve the stability of the final structure.

Sodium Lanthanide fluoride (NaLnF4) is an ideal matrix material with low phonon energy and high chemical stability [49]. In general, Ln3+ in NaLnF4 can be substituted by any atom of the lanthanide series, including Y3+, Gd3+, Eu3+, Tb3+, etc. As is well known, NaLnF4 is one of the most effective host material for upconversion (UC) emission, the low phonon energy is beneficial for improving energy transfer and luminescence efficiency, and its high chemical stability can maintain its structure and properties in various environments. Therefore, NaLnF4 has potential applications in the fields of bioimaging [50], fingerprinting [51], sensing [52,53], etc.

As is well known, smartphones’ image processing and color recognition technology and their widespread use, equipping smartphones with fluorescence nanosensor would facilitate rapid and low-cost detection in various aspects [54–56]. In order to improve the stability and practical applications of Hg2+ sensor, we elaborately constructed a smartphone assisted paper-based nanosensor for Hg2+ visual detection based on CDs and NaGdF4:Eu. In our present work, a dual-emissive nanocomposite was fabricated by incorporating CDs into mesoporous popcorn shape NaGdF4:Eu nanoparticles for quantitative and visual detection of Hg2+. The entire experiment process is shown in Scheme1 . First, popcorn shape NaGdF4:Eu as a matrix was obtained through a simple hydrothermal method. Then, CDs were successfully incorporated into the NaGdF4:Eu matrix by a hydrothermal treatment at 160℃ for 12 h with the use of Urea (UR) and citric acid (CA) as raw materials. The obtained NaGdF4:Eu3+/CDs solution showed blue emission without adding Hg2+. After adding a Hg2+ solution, the blue emission was gradually extinguished due to the coordination of functional groups on the surface of the CDs with Hg2+, and the red color fluorescence of NaGdF4:Eu gradually appeared. The target Hg2+ severely quenched the blue fluorescence of CDs centered at 470 nm but the red fluorescence of NaGdF4:Eu at 621nm remained almost constant, resulting in changes in the ratio fluorescence signals F470nm:F621nm in the sensing system. Therefore, the obtained NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite was further used to develop a ratiometric fluorescence probe for Hg2+ detection in aqueous solutions. The analysis results showed that the ratio fluorescence signals (F470nm:F621nm) were linearly related to the Hg2+ concentration in the range of 0.0 to 300 µM, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 20.8 nM. By impregnating the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs solution on filter paper, a low-cost portable test paper sensor can be constructed. Photographs of the test paper were taken by smartphone under a 254 nm UV lamp, the RGB value of the test paper was output with the help of color recognition scanning software in the smartphone, and a linear relation between the R/B value (y) and the Hg2+ concentration (x) was obtained. Furthermore, the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe and smartphone-based inspection platform were successfully applied to detect Hg2+ in actual water samples. and resource poor areas, as well as visual quantitative detection in the environment.

Europium chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O, 99.9 %) and gadoliniumchloridehexahydrate (GdCl3·6H2O, 99.9 %) were bought from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA, 99.5 %) was obtained from Hefei Qiansheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Urea (UR), an ammonium solution (25 % to 28 %), and other reagents were of Analytical Reagent.

The phase structure of the powder samples was detected by X-ray diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker Co., Ltd, GER) with Cu K-α radiation λ = 1.5418 Å. The morphologies of the samples were measured by a scanning electron microscope (Quanta 200F, FEI Co., Ltd, USA) and a transmission electron microscope (Tecnai G20, FEI Co., Ltd, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis was done using aphotoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250Xi, ThermoFisherScientific, USA). The photoluminescence properties were studied under fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The absorption spectra were obtained with a UV/VIS spectrometer (Agilent 8453, Agilent Technologies, Ltd, USA).

NaGdF4:Eu nanoparticles were prepared with the use of a hydrothermal method. Brifely, an aqueous solution of GdCl3 (18 mL, 0.4 M) and EuCl3 (2 mL, 0.4 M) was poured into a 100 mL flask containing sodium citrate (40 mL, 0.2 M) and stirred to produce a white precipitate. Then, NaF (1.64 g, 40 mM) was added to that mixture and the pH value was adjusted to 7 with ammonia. After continuously stirring for 30 min, the mixed solution was then poured into a 100 mL hydrothermal kettle and maintained at 180 ℃ for 2 h, then naturally cooled to room temperature. The product was separated by centrifugation (10,000 rpm,10 min) and washed twice with deionized water and ethanol, and finally dried at 60 ℃ for 24 h.

Typically, Urea (0.72 g) and CA (0.84g) were dissolved into 50 mL of deionized water, then the NaGdF4:Eu3+ precipitate (500 mg) was ultrasonically dispersed in the solution, then transferred into a 100 mL hydrothermal kettle, and maintained at 160 ℃ for 12 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the mixture was separated by centrifugation (10,000 rpm,10 min) and washed twice with ethanol and deionized water. Finally, the purified NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite was placed in an oven at 60 ℃ for 24 h.

For comparison, pure CDs were prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method: UR (0.72 g) and CA (0.84 g) were dissolved into 50 mL deionized water, then poured into a 100 mL hydrothermal kettle and maintained at 160 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was separated by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min). The supernatant was collected and purified through dialysis (10,000 molecular weight cutoff, 48 h) and further freeze-dried to obtain pure CDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The experiment for detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of Hg2+ was detected in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7). Various amounts of Hg2+ solution were added to the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs (10 mg/mL) PBS buffer solution. The final concentrations of Hg2+ ranged from 0 to 300 μM. Then, their fluorescence spectra were obtained under an excitation of 274 nm, and their fluorescence color was observed under a 254 nm UV lamp. All tests were conducted three parallel experiments.

A Hg2+ rapid detection test paper was directly prepared by soaking filter paper (with no back fluoresence) in a NaGdF4:Eu/CDs aqueous solution. A certain amount of Hg2+ with various concentrations were dropped onto the prepared test paper, and the naked-eye fluorescence discoloration was detected with a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp. Photographs were then taken with a smartphone. The color of the test paper was digitized and output with the help of the color recognition scanning software in the smartphone.

To study the selectivity of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite to Hg2+, a series of different metal ions (including K+, Na+, and Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Cr3+and Cr6+, Cd2+, and several anions (Cl-, Br-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2-) were added onto the test paper, and photographs were then taken with a smartphone under 254 nm ultraviolet lamp. The concentrations of the interfering substances and Hg2+ were both 300 µM. The anti-interference ability of the nanocomposites was evaluated by adding the above ions (500 µM) and Hg2+ (100 µM) to the test paper, at the same time.

50 mg NaGdF4: Eu/CDs were dispersed in 4 mL of ethanol solution, followed by 1 mL of glycerol as a thickener. After ultrasonic mixing for 10 min, a uniform and viscous anti-counterfeiting ink was formed. The anti-counterfeiting ink was directly written on filter paper (without background fluorescence).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase structure, morphology, and formation process
ce:section.ce:para: The composition and crystallinity of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite were determined by XRD (Fig. 1 A). Except for the appearance of a weak broad peak near 22°, which was similar to the diffraction peak of CDs reported in the literature [48], probably because a small number of amorphous CDs had a lower intensity than the well-crystallized NaGdF4. All the diffraction peaks were consistent with those of the standard card of hexagonal-phased NaGdF4 (JCPDS 27–0699).

The successful incorporation of CDs in the NaGdF4:Eu matrix was further determined by UV/VIS absorption spectroscopy (Fig. 1B), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1B shows the UV visible absorption spectrum of the obtained sample, and no prominent absorption peak was observed in the curve of NaGdF4:Eu. An absorption band near 350 nm was observed in the curve of CDs belonging to n → π* transitions of the C=O groups of the CDs [47]. In the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs curve, the CDs’ absorption peak near 350 nm was observed, indicating that the CDs were successfully loaded into NaGdF4:Eu3+. As shown in Fig. 1C, the full XPS spectrum of the CDs contained 3 peaks typical of CDs: C1s (284.7 eV), N1s (399.2eV), and O1s (531.2 eV). In the XPS spectrum of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite, in addition to the 3 peaks mentioned above, there were also peaks of F1s (681.2 eV), Na1s (1,070.5 eV), Gd3d (1,188.6 eV), and Gd3p (265.4 eV). The XPS spectra further demonstrated the presence of CDs in the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite.

The morphology and size of the as-synthesized NaGdF4:Eu, CDs, and NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite were investigated by SEM and TEM. The SEM image of NaGdF4:Eu (Fig. 1D) showed that the sample comprised well-dispersed popcorn shape nanoparticles with uniform diameters of approximately 150 nm. The morphology of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite maintained basically unchanged (Fig. 1E). The color mapping of Gadolinum (Gd), Flurine (F), Sodium (Na), Carbon (C), oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), Europium (Eu) elements (Fig. 1F, G, H, I, J, K, L) of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs showed that the O, C, N elements were detected in the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite. This result indicated that the carbon dots have been embedded into the NaGdF4:Eu matrix.

The TEM image of CDs was presented in Fig. 1 M, which consisted of a series of dark spots with diameters of approximately 3–5 nm. The TEM images of NaGdF4:Eu (Fig. 1N showed that every popcorn shape nanoparticle comprised several aggregated nanocrystallites. By comparing the light and dark areas of the NaGdF4:Eu nanoparticle, the mesoporous structure can be observed clearly, and those pores are suitable for embedding CDs. TEM image of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite (Fig. 1O) further indicated that the nanocomposite inherited the popcorn shape of the NaGdF4:Eu nanoparticle. By comparison, some black spots (circled in yellow) appeared in the space between the nanocrystallites, they were very similar to the spherical CDs.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the obtained CD aqueous solution emitted blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp, the highest excitation peak was near 370 nm, and the most substantial emission peak was centered at 470 nm. The photoluminescent spectra of the as-prepared NaGdF4:Eu are shown in Fig. 2B. The excitation spectrum was obtained at 621nm (5D0 → 7F2 energy level transition of Eu3+) as the monitoring wavelength. The excitation spectrum showed a strong excitation peak centered at 274 nm and a weak peak centered at 395 nm. Upon excitation at 274nm, NaGdF4:Eu showed an emission spectrum characteristic of Eu3+, including 596 nm (5D0→7F1) and 621 nm (5D0 → 7F2). The photograph in Fig. 2B shows that the NaGdF4:Eu solution was bright red under a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp.

The emission spectra of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite under excitation 274 nm and 395 nm were performed. Fig. 2C shows the emission spectrum of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite under excitation of 274 nm; it comprised a broad peak at approximately 470 nm and 2 sharp peaks at 596and 621 nm. Due to the stronger emission intensity of CDs in the solution compared with Eu3+, the solution showed blue fluorescence under a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp (as shown in inset of Fig. 2C). The emission spectrum of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite under excitation of 395 nm was presented in Fig.S1. The emission spectrum contained a broad emission peak of CD and two sharp peaks of Eu3+. However, compared with that at 274 nm, the emission of Eu3+ weaked greatly, making it unsuitable for visual detection. As is well known, the fluorescence of CD in solid state greatly decreases due to aggregation caused quenching (ACQ). However, the porous structure of the NaGdF4:Eu effectively prevented CD’s aggregation and avoided the quenching caused by CD aggregation. Figs. S2 show powder samples under a 365 nm and 254 nm ultraviolet lamp. The NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite emitted blue fluorescence at 365 nm, whereas, at 254 nm, pink fluorescence appeared. That result also showed that the CDs were uniformly embedded into the mesoporous popcorn shape NaGdF4:Eu.

The effect of Eu content on the fluorescence intensity of NaGdF4: Eu was investigated by changing the amount of EuCl3, The emission spectra of NaGdF4: Eu with different Eu contents were presented in Fig.S3. The results indicated that the fluorescence intensity is optimal when the Eu content is 10 %. At the same time, the fluorescence effects of urea and citric acid on NaGdF4: Eu/CD were studied by changing their contents. As shown in Fig.S4 and Table. S1, when the addition amount of NaGdF4: Eu is 500 mg, and the addition amounts of urea and citric acid are 0.72 g and 0.84 g, respectively, the emission at 470 nm and 621 nm reaches the optimum simultaneously. Therefore, NaGdF4: Eu/CD synthesized under optimal conditions was used for subsequent testing.

To improve the analytical performance of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs towards Hg2+, the effects of the solution concentration, pH value, reaction time, and temperature on the ratiometric fluorescence sensor were optimized and studied in an experiment. First of all, NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite aqueous solutions of various concentrations were prepared, and their emission spectra are shown in Fig. S5. It can be seen that the optimal emission appeared at the concentration of 10 mg/mL. A series of PBS solutions of various pHs (4.5 to 9.5) were prepared, and the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs was uniformly dispersed in the solution by ultrasound at a 10 mg/mL concentration. Fig. S6A shows the effect of the solution pH on the emission intensity ratio (F470nm:F621nm). It can be seen that when the pH of the system ranged from 4.5 and 9.5, the lowest value of F470nm:F621nm appeared at pH 7. That indicated that a pH value of 7 was the optimal pH environment. Therefore, a PBS buffer solution with pH 7 was selected in that method. When the pH of the system was 7, the result of the reaction-optimized temperature experiment is shown in Fig.S6B. It indicates that the lowest emission intensity ratio (F470nm:F621nm) appeared at 25℃ when the temperature ranged from 15 to 40℃. Therefore, this work was done at room temperature. The effect of time on the fluorescence ratio is shown in Fig. S6C. At the beginning, the emission intensity ratio (F470nm:F621nm) decreased markedly, then reached the nearly the lowest level after 5 min, and remained almost constant from then to 60 min. The results meant that the Hg2+ quenching reaction reached equilibrium within 5 min. Thus, in a follow-up experiment, the reaction time was 5 mins.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ detection by NaGdF4:Eu/CDs ratiometric fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of Hg2+ concentration on the fluorescence spectrum of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs ratiometric fluorescence probe was tested under the optimal experimental conditions. The fluorescence spectra were are presented in Fig. 2D. With the increase in Hg2+ concentration in the solution, the emission intensity at 470 nm decreased significantly. In contrast, the emission intensity at 621 nm remained almost constant, which meant that the emission intensity ratio (F470 nm:F621 nm) gradually decreased. As shown in Fig. 2E, there was a close linear relation between Hg2+ concentration (0 to 300 µM) and emission intensity ratio (F470nm:F621nm): y = − 0.00532x + 1.8518, and R2 = 0.9936, with an limit of detection (LOD) of 20.8 nM. The LOD was determined by utilizing the formula 3σ/k (where σ was the standard deviation from the blank solution during nine measurements and k was the slope of the calibration curve.

More importantly, with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the color of the solution was in gradual change from blue to red under a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp (as shown in the Fig. 2E inset). The above phenomena indicated that the prepared probe had great practical application potential in Hg2+ visual detection. Table.S2 shows that the proposed method had comparable detection range and LOD relative to those of other methods. Thus, that method showed great potential in practical applications.

First, in order to clarify the selectivity of the nanoprobes, a series of different metal ions (including K+, Na+ and Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Mn2+,Cr3+, Cr6+, Cd2+) and several anions (Cl-, Br-, NO3 –, CO3 2–, SO4 2-) are added to the test paper. The concentration of both the interfering substance and Hg2+ is 300 µM. As shown in Fig. 3 A and Fig. 3C, it can be seen that only when Hg2+ is added, the R/B value will increase significantly, and the effect of other ions is not significant. The anti-interference ability of the nanocomposites was evaluated by adding the above ions (500 µM) and Hg2+ (100 µM), respectively. Fig. 3B showed that the changes that occurred after the addition of other ions were negligible. From Fig.3C, it was found that the obtained nanoprobes show excellent selectivity (blue bar) and excellent anti-interference (purple bar) against Hg2+.Which was potentially due to the high affinity and the fast reaction kinetics of the nanocomposition towards Hg2+.

Based on the sensitive visual response of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs to Hg2+, a fast and low-cost smartphone-assisted Hg2+ real-time detection platform was developed. As shown in Fig. 3D, in the absence of Hg2+, the test paper displayed blue under a 254 nm ultraviolet lamp. However, when increasing concentrations of Hg2+ were added dropwise, the gradual transition from blue to red on the test paper could be clearly identified. A smartphone was used to capture the test paper images, and a color recognition program in the smartphone was used to identify the images’ red (R) and blue (B) color intensities. The R/B ratio was calculated to evaluate the fluorescence differences of the test paper under various concentrations of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 3E, the linear relation between the R/B value (y) and Hg2+ concentration (x) was obtained: y = 0.00177x + 0.2523, and the LOD for Hg2+ was calculated as 24.75 nM. Integrating the test paper with the smartphone simplified the Hg2+ detection process, making it cost-effective, portable, and real-time Hg2+ detection platform with great potential for practical applications.

Generally, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of fluorescent CDs can be summarized into four types: dynamic quenching (DQ), static quenching (SQ), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and inner filter effect (IFE) [57]. For further investigating the possible mechanism of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs to Hg2+, changes in UV spectrum (Fig. 4 A) and fluorescence lifetime (Fig. 4B) caused by the introduction of Hg2+ were measured. Due to the absence of an absorption peak for Hg2+ between 200–800 nm, there is no overlap between the UV absorption peak of Hg2+ and the excitation and emission peak of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs. Therefore, the possibility of FRET and IFE can be ruled out [58]. As shown in Fig. 4A, the absorption peak of the UV–vis absorption spectra of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs showed a significant red shift after the addition of Hg2+, because of the formation of new complex [59]. This result showed that the static quenching mechanism is involved in the quenching process. This is consistent with previous reports that Hg2+ exhibits strong coordination ability towards electron rich functional groups on the surface of CDs [28]. The fluorescence decay curve of the solution shows that the addition of Hg2+ has little effect on the fluorescence lifetime. The average lifetime of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs and NaGdF4:Eu/CDs + Hg2+ at 470 nm is 7.12 ns and 7.03 ns, respectively. This result further supports the static quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ detection in water samples by fluorescence spectrophotometer and smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: The real water spiked experiments were explored to verify the practicability and feasibility of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs for the detection of Hg2+. Tap water and lake water were selected for recovery experiments. After adding a certain amount of Hg2+, the reported ratio fluorescence probe and test paper platform were used to directly analyze the sample, and the concentraion of Hg2+ in the spiked sample was measured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-OES) for comparison. In addition, the mercury standard sample was also measured used our method. As listed in Table S3, the recoveries of NaGdF4:Eu/CDs solution varied in the range of 95.9 %- 101.6 % with an RSD of 0.16 %-2.40 %. In comparison, the test paper detected 94.1 %-102.9 % of the spiked content with an RSD of 0.47 %- 1.77 %. Both of the two methods could be capable of accurately quantifying CHg in real water samples.

We further studied the application of the NaGdF4:Eu/CDs nanocomposite in the field of anti-counterfeiting ink. The obtained anti-counterfeiting ink was used to write on the filter paper and apply on the butterfly, and then observed under the ultraviolet lamp. As shown in Fig.4C, the letters and butterfly were invisible in daylight (1 and 5) and blue fluorescence (2 and 6) under the 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, and after switching to 254 nm, the blue fluorescence was significantly weakened, and the red emission of Eu3+ appeared (3 and 7). When the Hg2+ solution is added to the filter paper, the red color is more obvious (4 and 8), due to the quench effect of blue emission of CDs.

In order to improve the stability of Hg2+ sensor and the convenience of on-site detection, NaGdF4: Eu, a chemically stable fluorescent host material, was used as a matrix, and CD was used as a fluorescent element. A dual-emissive NaGdF4: Eu/CDs nanocomposite material was conveniently synthesized by a one-step in-situ hydrothermal method. The obtained NaGdF4: Eu/CDs nanocomposite exhibits good performance in detecting Hg2+ in real water. A ratio fluorescent probe for Hg2+ detection has been developed, with a linear range of 0 to 300 µ M and a LOD of 20.8 nM, which is lower than the limit mentioned by the World Health Organization (WHO). At the same time, with the help of smartphones, a fast, low-cost, and real-time detection platform has been developed for Hg2+ in wild and resource poor areas, as well as visual quantitative detection in the environment. In addition, the obtained NaGdF4: Eu/CDs nanocomposite material shows good application prospects in anti-counterfeiting.

Xiaogao Guan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Xianzhu Xu: Software, Methodology. Qiyue Zhang: Investigation. Yanli Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xingcong Wang: Software, Methodology. Xi Chen: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Qiang Xiao: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this study by Jiangxi Province Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules (No: 2024SSY05141)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112978.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
